title: Make an LED breathe
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Keep yesterday's single-LED circuit, upload the Arduino sketch, and watch the light fade up to full and back down to dark on a loop. The fade is the part you see; the real lesson is how a pin that only knows on and off produces every brightness in between — by switching far too fast to watch and staying on for a bigger or smaller share of each instant. That one trick, PWM, runs dimmers, motor speeds, and servos for the rest of the course.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for a breathing LED: a single LED on GPIO 2 through a 220 ohm resistor to ground, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: LED
parts.explanation: A LED is a one-way light. It only lights when its longer leg (the +) faces the pin and its shorter leg (the −) faces ground. Too much current burns it out, so a LED always sits behind a resistor.

parts.name: 220 Ω resistor
parts.explanation: A resistor is a small component whose whole job is to limit how much current flows through a wire. Current is the flow of electricity through the circuit; a resistor throttles that flow down to a safe amount. Its strength is measured in ohms (Ω) — a larger number resists harder and lets less current through. A part like a LED wired straight to power pulls far more current than it can survive and burns out at once, so it sits in series with a resistor whose value is chosen to hold the current gentle enough to keep it alive. The coloured bands print the resistor's value, and it works the same either way round — a resistor has no positive or negative end.

parts.name: Jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: This pin fades the LED with PWM.
Completes the LED's path back to zero volts.

coachInstructions: Let the learner see the LED breathe first, then land the mechanism- a pin is only ever on or off, so PWM fakes every in-between brightness by switching fast and holding on for a bigger or smaller share of each cycle.
Separate the two dials clearly- frequency is how fast it switches (1000 Hz, just past what the eye can follow) and duty cycle is the share that's on, which is the brightness.
Point out that the ledc hardware does the switching- the pin is attached to a channel once in setup(), then ledcWrite() just names a level from 0 to 255 while the chip keeps pulsing on its own.
Confirm this is the same single-LED circuit as Blink — LED direction and the 220 Ω series resistor are the usual first mistakes.
If the LED snaps hard on and off instead of fading, suspect the Blink sketch is still loaded and have them re-upload 04.1.
If the LED stays dim or dark, check the 220 Ω resistor, the GPIO 2 wire, and the LED's direction before changing code.
For the slow-frequency experiment, always work on a copy- drop FRQ to about 4 and hold one fixed ledcWrite so the flicker is visible, then restore FRQ 1000 and the fade loops.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up, and its 0–1023 duty is expected to differ from Arduino's 0–255.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Place the LED so its long leg (+) is on the GPIO 2 side and its short leg (−) heads toward ground.
Put the 220 Ω resistor in series between GPIO 2 and the LED's long leg.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_04.1_BreathingLight.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Watch the LED fade up and down, over and over.

arduino.explanation: Hands GPIO 2 to one of the board's hardware PWM channels at 1000 Hz with 8-bit resolution, so from here the chip does the fast switching itself.
Sets the duty cycle from 0 (never on, dark) to 255 (always on, full) — the share of each cycle the pin holds on, which your eye reads as brightness.
The first loop walks the duty up and the second walks it back down; delay(10) sets how long each of the steps lingers, so it sets the breathing speed.

micropython.explanation: Sets up PWM on GPIO 2 at 10 kHz — the same fast-switching trick the Arduino sketch uses.
Sets brightness by duty, which is how much of each instant the pin is on.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: A pin can only be fully on or fully off, so PWM fakes every middle brightness by switching fast and holding on for a chosen share of each cycle.
theoryModel.formula: brightness = duty cycle = the share of each fast cycle the pin holds on

notes.title: Two dials, and only one is brightness
notes.body: Frequency and duty cycle are separate settings. Frequency is how many on-off cycles happen each second (1000 here) and only has to be fast enough to beat your eye. Duty cycle is the share of each cycle that is on, and that one alone sets how bright it looks.

notes.title: Set it once, then walk away
notes.body: You attach the pin to a channel a single time in setup(). PWM_BIT is 8, so each cycle is split into 256 slices and the level 0 to 255 chooses how many are on. After that the ledc hardware keeps pulsing on its own while ledcWrite() just names a new level.

expectedOutputExample: The LED brightens smoothly from dark up to full.
Then it dims smoothly back down to dark.
It repeats, over and over, like slow breathing.
test.successCriteria: The change should be a smooth slide, never a hard on/off snap. If it snaps, you're likely still running the Blink sketch.

troubleshooting.symptom: LED just blinks hard on and off
firstChecks: You may be on the Blink sketch — re-upload Sketch_04.1_BreathingLight.
Check the 220 Ω resistor and the GPIO 2 wire, then the LED's direction.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Drop the PWM frequency to a few hertz and hold one fixed brightness so the switching becomes a visible blink, then change the level and watch the on-flash grow and shrink — duty cycle made tangible.
